label: Formula 19.7d
section: ANIMAL-MODEL ANALYSIS OF SELECTION EXPERIMENTS
nearby_text: the fixed effects estimates, $\mathbf{C}_{11}$, the predictor errors for the BLUPs of breeding values, $\mathbf{C}_{22}$, and the covariances, $\mathbf{C}_{12}$, between these estimates are given by $$ \begin{pmatrix}\mathbf{C}_{11}&\mathbf{C}_{12}\\\mathbf{C}_{12}^{T}&\mathbf{C}_{22}\end{pmatrix}=\begin{pmatrix}n&\mathbf{1}^{T}\\\mathbf{1}&\mathbf{I}+\lambda\mathbf{A}^{-1}\end{pmatrix}^{-1} $$ (19.7d)

label: Formula 19.11
section: ANIMAL-MODEL ANALYSIS OF SELECTION EXPERIMENTS
nearby_text: Common-environment effects are incorporated into the basic animal model by simply adding a fixed effect, $ b_{t} $, for the common environmental effect in generation t $$ y_{t k}=\mu+b_{t}+a_{t k}+e_{t k} $$ (19.11)

label: Formula 19.12a
section: ANIMAL-MODEL ANALYSIS OF SELECTION EXPERIMENTS
nearby_text: vector of all observations from line $k$. If the generational environmental effects are assumed to be the same across the lines, the model for the $i$th individual in generation $t$ from line $k$ is $$ y_{k,ti}=\mu+b_{t}+a_{k,ti}+e_{k,ti} $$ (19.12a)

subsection: Estimating the Additive Variance at Generation t
nearby_text: Another potential approach to estimating the additive variance in generation t would be to use the variance among the predicted breeding values within a generation $$ \mathrm{Var}(A_{t})=\frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n_{t}}\left(\widehat{a}_{ti}-\widehat{\overline{a}}_{t}\right)^{2} $$ (19.14)

nearby_text: t in generation $ t $ (Equation 16.9c). label: Formula 19.21a
section: MODIFICATIONS OF THE BASIC ANIMAL MODEL
nearby_text: The resulting set of Henderson’s mixed-model equations (Equation 19.4) for the model specified by Equations 19.20a and 19.20b becomes $$ \begin{pmatrix}\mathbf{X}^{T}\mathbf{X}&\mathbf{X}^{T}\mathbf{Z}&\mathbf{X}^{T}\mathbf{W}\\\mathbf{Z}^{T}\mathbf{X}&\mathbf{Z}^{T}\mathbf{Z}+\lambda_{A}\mathbf{A}^{-1}&\mathbf{Z}^{T}\mathbf{W}\\\mathbf{W}^{T}\mathbf{X}&\mathbf{W}^{T}\mathbf{Z}&\mathbf{W}^{T}\mathbf{W}+\lambda_{u}\mathbf{I}\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}\widehat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}\\\widehat{\mathbf{a}}\\\widehat{\mathbf{u}}\end{pmatrix}=\begin{pmatrix}\mathbf{X}^{T}\mathbf{y}\\\mathbf{Z}^{T}\mathbf{y}\\\mathbf{W}^{T}\mathbf{y}\end{pmatrix} $$ (19.21a) where $$ \lambda_{A}=\frac{\sigma_{e}^{2}}{\sigma_{A}^{2}}\quad and\quad\lambda_{u}=\frac{\sigma_{e}^{2}}{\sigma_{u}^{2}} $$ (19.21b)

label: Formula 19.23
section: MODIFICATIONS OF THE BASIC ANIMAL MODEL
subsection: Treating Certain Breeding Values as Fixed Effects
nearby_text: lues of 1/2 in the parent's column in each row. Here, $ a_r $ is treated as a random effect because it is a function of a fixed effect ( $ a_b $) and a random effect (s). The resulting mixed-model is $$ \mathbf{y}=\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}+\mathbf{Z}_{1}\mathbf{a}_{\mathbf{b}}+\mathbf{Z}_{2}\mathbf{a}_{\mathbf{r}}+\mathbf{e} $$ (19.23)

label: Formula 19.25a
section: MODIFICATIONS OF THE BASIC ANIMAL MODEL
nearby_text: uming no common-family environmental effects (which is a major assumption), we can attempt to estimate dominance as follows. Letting the vector d denote the dominance effects, the mixed model becomes $$ \mathbf{y}=\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}+\mathbf{Z}\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{Z}\mathbf{d}+\mathbf{e} $$ (19.25a)

Note that D is expected to be considerably more sparse (most of its off-diagonal elements are zero) than A, and hence may"

nearby_text: cause $ p(\mathbf{y}) $, the probability of the data vector $ \mathbf{y} $, is a constant independent of $ \boldsymbol{\Theta} $, it is typically ignored, and the posterior is often simply written as $$ p(\boldsymbol{\Theta}\mid\mathbf{y})\propto p(\mathbf{y}\mid\boldsymbol{\Theta})p(\boldsymbol{\Theta}) $$ (19.29b)

label: Formula 19.30
section: BAYESIAN MIXED-MODEL ANALYSIS
nearby_text: vector of nuisance variables. Integrating the full posterior over $ \boldsymbol{\Theta}_{nu} $ yields the marginal posterior distribution for the variables of interest, $ \boldsymbol{\Theta}_1 $, as $$ \begin{aligned}p(\boldsymbol{\Theta}_{1}\mid\mathbf{y})&=\int p(\boldsymbol{\Theta}_{1},\boldsymbol{\Theta}_{nu}\mid\mathbf{y})d\boldsymbol{\Theta}_{nu}\\&=\int p(\boldsymbol{\Theta}_{1}\mid\boldsymbol{\Theta}_{nu},\mathbf{y})p(\boldsymbol{\Theta}_{nu}\mid\mathbf{y})d\boldsymbol{\Theta}_{nu}\\&=E_{\boldsymbol{\Theta}_{nu}}\left[p(\boldsymbol{\Theta}_{1}\mid\boldsymbol{\Theta}_{nu},\mathbf{y})\right]\\ \end{aligned} $$ (19.30)

label: Formula 19.32a
section: BAYESIAN MIXED-MODEL ANALYSIS
subsection: Computing Posteriors and Marginals: MCMC and the Gibbs Sampler
nearby_text: Any feature of interest for the marginals can be computed from the m realizations of the Gibbs sequence. For example, the expectation of any function, f, of the random variable, x, is approximated by $$ E[f(x)]_{m}=\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}f(x_{i}) $$ (19.32a) which is simply the average of the function evaluated over the points in the sampler.   "nearby_text": "Any feature of interest for the marginals can be computed from the m realizations of the Gibbs sequence. For example, the expectation of any function, f, of the random variable, x, is approximated by $$ E[f(x)]_{m}=\\frac{1}{m}\\sum_{i=1}^{m}f(x_{i}) $$ (19.32a) which is simply the average of the function evaluated over the points in the sampler. This is the Monte Carlo (MC) estimate of $ f(x) $, as $ E[f(x)]_m \\to E[f(x)] $ as $ m \\to \\infty $. Likewise, th"

label: Formula 19.32b
section: BAYESIAN MIXED-MODEL ANALYSIS
subsection: Computing Posteriors and Marginals: MCMC and the Gibbs Sampler
nearby_text: e Carlo (MC) estimate of $ f(x) $, as $ E[f(x)]_m \to E[f(x)] $ as $ m \to \infty $. Likewise, the MC estimate for any function of $ n $ variables $ (\theta^{(1)}, \cdots, \theta^{(n)}) $ is given by $$ E[f(\theta^{(1)},\cdots,\theta^{(n)})]_{m}=\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}f(\theta^{(1)}_{i},\cdots,\theta^{(n)}_{i}) $$ (19.32b)

label: Formula 19.33a
section: BAYESIAN MIXED-MODEL ANALYSIS
subsection: Bayesian Analysis of the Animal Model
nearby_text: ensions. As before, the conditional distribution of the data, given the vectors of fixed effects, $ \beta $, breeding values, a, and the environmental variance, $ \sigma_e^2 $, is multivariate normal $$ \mathbf{y}\mid\boldsymbol{\beta},\mathbf{a},\sigma_{e}^{2}\sim\mathrm{MVN}(\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}+\mathbf{Z}\mathbf{a},\sigma_{e}^{2}\mathbf{I}) $$ (19.33a)

label: Formula 19.33b
section: BAYESIAN MIXED-MODEL ANALYSIS
subsection: Bayesian Analysis of the Animal Model
nearby_text: The infinitesimal model is assumed, with the distribution of breeding values, given the relationship matrix, A, and additive genetic variance, $ \sigma_{A}^{2} $, being multivariate normal $$ \mathbf{a}\mid\mathbf{A},\sigma_{A}^{2}\sim\mathrm{MVN}(\mathbf{0},\sigma_{A}^{2}\mathbf{A}) $$ (19.33b)
